topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee in my mug felt suddenly cold, the ceramic slick against my palms. I stared at the photograph—us, grinning wildly on a beach trip from seventh grade—and the edges of the paper seemed to blur. Liam, my constant, my anchor through all the awkwardness and triumphs of growing up, was sitting across from me, his expression one of genuine, warm familiarity. But when I tried to recall the specifics of that summer, the details dissolved. I tried to pull up a memory, any scrap of shared history, but the file was stubbornly blank. A strange hollowness bloomed in my chest, a physical pressure that made my breath catch. I finally managed to speak, the sound thin and reedy. "Do you… do you remember that trip to the coast?" His brow furrowed, a flicker of confusion crossing his face, quickly replaced by a gentle, puzzled tilt of his head. He looked at me with kindness, but behind that kindness, I sensed a vast, uncrossable distance. My hands began to tremble, and I quickly lowered the picture, tucking it into my pocket as if it were suddenly radioactive.

[story 2]

I walked through the familiar hallways of the old school, the linoleum floors echoing the rhythm of my anxious footsteps. Every corner held a ghost of shared laughter, a phantom presence of Liam. I stopped by his locker, the metal cool beneath my fingertips, and tried to conjure the easy cadence of our conversations. "Hey, remember that time we tried to build that ridiculous fort in the library?" I started, my voice pitched a little too high. Liam turned, his eyes wide and open, taking in my frantic energy. He offered a small, polite smile, the kind you give a stranger you’ve just encountered. "I don't recall that, sorry," he said, his tone perfectly neutral, utterly devoid of the warmth I desperately craved. The air seemed to thin around me. I felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in my stomach, like I’d just jumped off a high ledge. I took a step back, my feet feeling unsteady on the floor, and mumbled something about needing to find my history textbook, turning abruptly and walking away before I could let the tremor in my hands show.

[story 3]

The silence in the park bench was immense, pressing in on me like deep water. I watched Liam across the green expanse, talking animatedly with another group of friends. It was agonizingly easy for him to move through the world, connecting with people effortlessly. I watched him, cataloging every gesture, searching for a spark, a recognition of the years we’d spent intertwined. Nothing. He laughed at something one of them said, a bright, uncomplicated sound, and I felt a sharp, internal withdrawal. My jaw tightened, and I gripped the strap of my bag until my knuckles were white. When he finally glanced my way, his eyes met mine for a fleeting second. There was acknowledgment there, yes, but no resonance. It was like watching a film where I was the only person who had been erased from the credits. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I focused intently on the pattern of the leaves on the tree, needing the solid, predictable texture of something tangible to ground me as the reality of the situation settled in, heavy and undeniable.
